sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 17-year-old non-binary individual, presents with a newly diagnosed elevated cholesterol level.

The patient was referred to the clinic after a finger prick test revealed a cholesterol level of 210, which was described as "elevated." The patient reports being concerned about this finding and wanting to understand what it means.
The patient denies any significant past medical history and reports a good relationship with their grandparents, who do not have a history of hypercholesterolemia.
The patient has a history of being non-binary and reports feeling comfortable with this identity.
The patient's grandparents have a family history of a cousin who died of a heart attack at the age of 44 or 45.
The patient denies smoking or being exposed to secondhand smoke.
The patient reports being active, enjoying bike riding, and having a diet that is mostly clean with occasional indulgences in chocolate chip cookies.
Review of systems: The patient denies any issues with growth or development, including bone growth or feeling like they are not healthy.
Past medical history: The patient has no significant past medical history, and their grandparents do not have a history of hypercholesterolemia.
The patient's cousin died of a heart attack at the age of 44 or 45.
Review of systems: The patient denies any issues with growth or development, including bone growth or feeling like they are not healthy.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure was found to be normal during the physical exam.
Physical exam: The patient's heart exam, lung exam, eye exam, and neck exam were all within normal limits.
The patient's abdominal exam was also normal, with no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly.
Test Results: The patient's cholesterol level was found to be elevated at 210.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a newly diagnosed elevated cholesterol level, which is a risk factor for premature heart attacks.
Problem list: Hypercholesterolemia; Suspected familial hypercholesterolemia.
Plan:
I prescribed a full cholesterol panel to investigate the cause of the elevated cholesterol level.
I also ordered a complete metabolic panel, thyroid studies, and liver panels to rule out secondary causes.
I performed genetic testing to determine if the hypercholesterolemia is familial.
I recommended a well-balanced diet, including a variety of foods that are lower in saturated fat and sugars, and referred the patient to a nutritionist to provide guidance on dietary changes.
I advised the patient to continue their active lifestyle, including bike riding, and to follow up with another cholesterol level in 3-6 months after implementing dietary changes.
Follow-up: I plan to follow up with the patient in 3-6 months to check on their progress and recheck their cholesterol level.
